It is a keyhole shaped Kofun.<ref name="mw-2023a">{{Cite web |date=2023-10-16 |title=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun [纏向石塚古墳] Long Barrow : The Megalithic Portal and Megalith Map |url=https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231016234953/https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |archive-date=2023-10-16 }}</ref> It is a Kofun of [[Makimuku ruins]].<ref name="Ki11er-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Ki11er |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.1515/9780824862848 |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>{{rp|249}}
During WWII it was flattened to use as a site for an {{ill|Anti-aircraft warfare|en|Anti-aircraft warfare|WD=Q86739852|lt=Anti-aircraft cannon}}.<ref name="mw-2023a" />'
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} and one of the most prominent tombs of its time.<ref name="Ishino-1992a">{{Cite journal |last1=Ishino |first1=Hironobu |last2=石野博信 |date=1992 |title=Rites and Rituals of the Kofun Period |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=19 |issue=2/3 |pages=191–216 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.19.2-3.1992.191-216 |jstor=30234190 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref>
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is 92 meters long. It dates back to the time of [[Himiko]], who led the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamatai kingdom]] in Japan. [[Himiko]] made connections with the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei court}} in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. She was important in changing {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious}} and ritual practices.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
It is dated to around 180CE, with the nearby {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} being dated to around {{ill|200|en|200|WD=Q23813|lt=200AD}}.<ref>https://seaa-web.org/sites/default/files/publications/bseaa-1/BSEAA1-Rew-Styles.pdf</ref>
It was the first Kofun excavated from [[Makimuku ruins]],<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a">{{Cite web |title=State Formation in Japan: Emergence of a 4th-Century Ruling Elite |url=https://www.routledge.com/State-Formation-in-Japan-Emergence-of-a-4th-Century-Ruling-Elite/Barnes/p/book/9780415596282 |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=115}} and was where some of the earliest {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} was found.<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a" />{{Rp|page=115–116}}
== Religious significance == [[File:Shinshi 001.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Chickens at [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Parishioners believe they are {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=messengers}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="D.-2011">{{Cite web |last=D. |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="Ise Shrine-2013">{{Cite web |date=2013-02-15 |title=Ise Shrine (Ise Jingu) - Ise, Japan |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130215040040/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-date=2013-02-15 |access-date=2023-11-11}}</ref><ref name="Olderr-2017">{{Cite book |last=Olderr |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y5gZDgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA171 |title=Symbolism: A Comprehensive Dictionary, 2d ed. |date=2017-02-10 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9067-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Goto-2015">{{Cite book |last1=Goto |first1=Seiko |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VN29CgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA132 |title=Japanese Gardens: Symbolism and Design |last2=Naka |first2=Takahiro |date=2015-10-16 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-41164-2 |language=en}}</ref>]] {{ill|Archaeological record|en|Archaeological record|WD=Q2686349|lt=Archaeological evidence}} indicates rituals were performed in the tomb. Wooden {{ill|Column|en|Column|WD=Q4817|lt=pillars}} 20 cm in diameter were erected within its moats.<ref>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/prepareDownload?itemId=info%3Andljp%2Fpid%2F9591536&contentNo=1</ref><ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Wooden {{ill|Chicken|en|Chicken|WD=Q780|lt=roosters}} painted in vermilion lacquer were discovered in the moat surrounding the mound's circular section.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The bird carving is 39 centimeters long.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}} Historians and archaeologists believe these [[:en:roosters|roosters]] might have been part of [[:en:ceremonies|ceremonies]] aimed at praying for the {{ill|Resurrection|en|Resurrection|WD=Q188681|lt=Resurrection}} of the deceased. Similar wooden and clay figures resembling roosters have been found in other early [[Kofun period]] tombs.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Roosters are the {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=animal messengers}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
The birds may symbolize the {{ill|Metempsychosis|en|Metempsychosis|WD=Q1262856|lt=soul's journey}}. These items were found in peat at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-sone site}} in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi City}}, Osaka. The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}
The tomb faces {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}, a sacred mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The [[Kojiki]], says the god [[Ōmononushi]] wanted people to worship him on this mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> There is a complex narrative about {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} and the establishment of worship in the region.<ref name="Ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}} with some interpreting the god as being {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}} one of the two gods previously worshipped in the Imperial palace alongside [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="Ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="Hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}} The tomb's direction shows that the Makimuku area's rulers and people respected {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} deeply. Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=religious link}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} * [[Hokenoyama Kofun]]

'''Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun''' is a [[Kofun]] found on the premises of [[Makimuku ruins]]. It is a keyhole shaped Kofun.<ref name="mw-2023a">{{Cite web |date=2023-10-16 |title=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun [纏向石塚古墳] Long Barrow : The Megalithic Portal and Megalith Map |url=https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231016234953/https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |archive-date=2023-10-16 }}</ref> It is a Kofun of [[Makimuku ruins]].<ref name="Ki11er-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Ki11er |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.1515/9780824862848 |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>{{rp|249}}
During WWII it was flattened to use as a site for an {{ill|Anti-aircraft warfare|en|Anti-aircraft warfare|WD=Q86739852|lt=Anti-aircraft cannon}}.<ref name="mw-2023a" />'
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} and one of the most prominent tombs of its time.<ref name="Ishino-1992a">{{Cite journal |last1=Ishino |first1=Hironobu |last2=石野博信 |date=1992 |title=Rites and Rituals of the Kofun Period |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=19 |issue=2/3 |pages=191–216 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.19.2-3.1992.191-216 |jstor=30234190 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref>
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is 92 meters long. It dates back to the time of [[Himiko]], who led the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamatai kingdom]] in Japan. [[Himiko]] made connections with the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei court}} in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. She was important in changing {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious}} and ritual practices.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
It is dated to around 180CE, with the nearby {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} being dated to around {{ill|200|en|200|WD=Q23813|lt=200AD}}.<ref>https://seaa-web.org/sites/default/files/publications/bseaa-1/BSEAA1-Rew-Styles.pdf</ref>
It was the first Kofun excavated from [[Makimuku ruins]],<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a">{{Cite web |title=State Formation in Japan: Emergence of a 4th-Century Ruling Elite |url=https://www.routledge.com/State-Formation-in-Japan-Emergence-of-a-4th-Century-Ruling-Elite/Barnes/p/book/9780415596282 |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=115}} and was where some of the earliest {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} was found.<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a" />{{Rp|page=115–116}}

Parishioners believe they are {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=messengers}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="D.-2011">{{Cite web |last=D. |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="Ise Shrine-2013">{{Cite web |date=2013-02-15 |title=Ise Shrine (Ise Jingu) - Ise, Japan |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130215040040/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-date=2013-02-15 |access-date=2023-11-11}}</ref><ref name="Olderr-2017">{{Cite book |last=Olderr |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y5gZDgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA171 |title=Symbolism: A Comprehensive Dictionary, 2d ed. |date=2017-02-10 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9067-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Goto-2015">{{Cite book |last1=Goto |first1=Seiko |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VN29CgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA132 |title=Japanese Gardens: Symbolism and Design |last2=Naka |first2=Takahiro |date=2015-10-16 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-41164-2 |language=en}}</ref>]] {{ill|Archaeological record|en|Archaeological record|WD=Q2686349|lt=Archaeological evidence}} indicates rituals were performed in the tomb. Wooden {{ill|Column|en|Column|WD=Q4817|lt=pillars}} 20 cm in diameter were erected within its moats.<ref>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/prepareDownload?itemId=info%3Andljp%2Fpid%2F9591536&contentNo=1</ref><ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Wooden {{ill|Chicken|en|Chicken|WD=Q780|lt=roosters}} painted in vermilion lacquer were discovered in the moat surrounding the mound's circular section.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The bird carving is 39 centimeters long.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}} Historians and archaeologists believe these [[:en:roosters|roosters]] might have been part of [[:en:ceremonies|ceremonies]] aimed at praying for the {{ill|Resurrection|en|Resurrection|WD=Q188681|lt=Resurrection}} of the deceased. The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}

Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=religious link}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />

It was the first Kofun excavated from [[Makimuku ruins]],<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a">{{Cite web |title=State Formation in Japan: Emergence of a 4th-Century Ruling Elite |url=https://www.routledge.com/State-Formation-in-Japan-Emergence-of-a-4th-Century-Ruling-Elite/Barnes/p/book/9780415596282 |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=115}} and was where some of the earliest {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} was found.<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a" />{{Rp|page=115–116}}
== Religious significance == [[File:Shinshi 001.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Chickens at [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Parishioners believe they are {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=messengers}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="D.-2011">{{Cite web |last=D. |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="Ise Shrine-2013">{{Cite web |date=2013-02-15 |title=Ise Shrine (Ise Jingu) - Ise, Japan |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130215040040/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-date=2013-02-15 |access-date=2023-11-11}}</ref><ref name="Olderr-2017">{{Cite book |last=Olderr |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y5gZDgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA171 |title=Symbolism: A Comprehensive Dictionary, 2d ed. |date=2017-02-10 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9067-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Goto-2015">{{Cite book |last1=Goto |first1=Seiko |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VN29CgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA132 |title=Japanese Gardens: Symbolism and Design |last2=Naka |first2=Takahiro |date=2015-10-16 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-41164-2 |language=en}}</ref>]] {{ill|Archaeological record|en|Archaeological record|WD=Q2686349|lt=Archaeological evidence}} indicates rituals were performed in the tomb. Wooden {{ill|Column|en|Column|WD=Q4817|lt=pillars}} 20 cm in diameter were erected within its moats.<ref>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/prepareDownload?itemId=info%3Andljp%2Fpid%2F9591536&contentNo=1</ref><ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Wooden {{ill|Chicken|en|Chicken|WD=Q780|lt=roosters}} painted in vermilion lacquer were discovered in the moat surrounding the mound's circular section.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The bird carving is 39 centimeters long.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}} Historians and archaeologists believe these [[:en:roosters|roosters]] might have been part of [[:en:ceremonies|ceremonies]] aimed at praying for the {{ill|Resurrection|en|Resurrection|WD=Q188681|lt=Resurrection}} of the deceased. Similar wooden and clay figures resembling roosters have been found in other early [[Kofun period]] tombs.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Roosters are the {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=animal messengers}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
The birds may symbolize the {{ill|Metempsychosis|en|Metempsychosis|WD=Q1262856|lt=soul's journey}}. These items were found in peat at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-sone site}} in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi City}}, Osaka. The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}
The tomb faces {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}, a sacred mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The [[Kojiki]], says the god [[Ōmononushi]] wanted people to worship him on this mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> There is a complex narrative about {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} and the establishment of worship in the region.<ref name="Ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}} with some interpreting the god as being {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}} one of the two gods previously worshipped in the Imperial palace alongside [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="Ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="Hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}} The tomb's direction shows that the Makimuku area's rulers and people respected {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} deeply. Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=religious link}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} * [[Hokenoyama Kofun]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Ancient Kofun in Japan}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}}| image=File:Makimuku,ishidukakofun.JPG |coordinates={{Coord|34.54625|N|135.836083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |caption=The three tumuli are surrounded by the Makimuku Katsuyama tumulus (top), the Yatsuka tumulus (bottom left) and the Ishizuka tumulus (bottom right), and the Makimuku Elementary School is located between them.|location={{ill|Sakurai|WD=Q127026}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11608504}}}}
'''Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun''' is a [[Kofun]] found on the premises of [[Makimuku ruins]]. It is a keyhole shaped Kofun.<ref name="mw-2023a">{{Cite web |date=2023-10-16 |title=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun [纏向石塚古墳] Long Barrow : The Megalithic Portal and Megalith Map |url=https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231016234953/https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |archive-date=2023-10-16 }}</ref> It is a Kofun of [[Makimuku ruins]].<ref name="Ki11er-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Ki11er |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.1515/9780824862848 |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>{{rp|249}}
During WWII it was flattened to use as a site for an {{ill|Anti-aircraft warfare|en|Anti-aircraft warfare|WD=Q86739852|lt=Anti-aircraft cannon}}.<ref name="mw-2023a" />'
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} and one of the most prominent tombs of its time.<ref name="Ishino-1992a">{{Cite journal |last1=Ishino |first1=Hironobu |last2=石野博信 |date=1992 |title=Rites and Rituals of the Kofun Period |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=19 |issue=2/3 |pages=191–216 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.19.2-3.1992.191-216 |jstor=30234190 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref>
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is 92 meters long. It dates back to the time of [[Himiko]], who led the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamatai kingdom]] in Japan. [[Himiko]] made connections with the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei court}} in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. She was important in changing {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious}} and ritual practices.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
It is dated to around 180CE, with the nearby {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} being dated to around {{ill|200|en|200|WD=Q23813|lt=200AD}}.<ref>https://seaa-web.org/sites/default/files/publications/bseaa-1/BSEAA1-Rew-Styles.pdf</ref>
It was the first Kofun excavated from [[Makimuku ruins]],<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a">{{Cite web |title=State Formation in Japan: Emergence of a 4th-Century Ruling Elite |url=https://www.routledge.com/State-Formation-in-Japan-Emergence-of-a-4th-Century-Ruling-Elite/Barnes/p/book/9780415596282 |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=115}} and was where some of the earliest {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} was found.<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a" />{{Rp|page=115–116}}
== Religious significance == [[File:Shinshi 001.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Chickens at [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Parishioners believe they are {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=messengers}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="D.-2011">{{Cite web |last=D. |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="Ise Shrine-2013">{{Cite web |date=2013-02-15 |title=Ise Shrine (Ise Jingu) - Ise, Japan |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130215040040/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-date=2013-02-15 |access-date=2023-11-11}}</ref><ref name="Olderr-2017">{{Cite book |last=Olderr |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y5gZDgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA171 |title=Symbolism: A Comprehensive Dictionary, 2d ed. |date=2017-02-10 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9067-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Goto-2015">{{Cite book |last1=Goto |first1=Seiko |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VN29CgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA132 |title=Japanese Gardens: Symbolism and Design |last2=Naka |first2=Takahiro |date=2015-10-16 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-41164-2 |language=en}}</ref>]] {{ill|Archaeological record|en|Archaeological record|WD=Q2686349|lt=Archaeological evidence}} indicates rituals were performed in the tomb. Wooden {{ill|Column|en|Column|WD=Q4817|lt=pillars}} 20 cm in diameter were erected within its moats.<ref>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/prepareDownload?itemId=info%3Andljp%2Fpid%2F9591536&contentNo=1</ref><ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Wooden {{ill|Chicken|en|Chicken|WD=Q780|lt=roosters}} painted in vermilion lacquer were discovered in the moat surrounding the mound's circular section.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The bird carving is 39 centimeters long.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}} Historians and archaeologists believe these [[:en:roosters|roosters]] might have been part of [[:en:ceremonies|ceremonies]] aimed at praying for the {{ill|Resurrection|en|Resurrection|WD=Q188681|lt=Resurrection}} of the deceased. Similar wooden and clay figures resembling roosters have been found in other early [[Kofun period]] tombs.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Roosters are the {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=animal messengers}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
The birds may symbolize the {{ill|Metempsychosis|en|Metempsychosis|WD=Q1262856|lt=soul's journey}}. These items were found in peat at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-sone site}} in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi City}}, Osaka. The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}
The tomb faces {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}, a sacred mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The [[Kojiki]], says the god [[Ōmononushi]] wanted people to worship him on this mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> There is a complex narrative about {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} and the establishment of worship in the region.<ref name="Ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}} with some interpreting the god as being {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}} one of the two gods previously worshipped in the Imperial palace alongside [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="Ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="Hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}} The tomb's direction shows that the Makimuku area's rulers and people respected {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} deeply. Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=religious link}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} * [[Hokenoyama Kofun]]

[[Himiko]] made connections with the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei court}} in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}. She was important in changing {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious}} and ritual practices.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
It is dated to around 180CE, with the nearby {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} being dated to around {{ill|200|en|200|WD=Q23813|lt=200AD}}.<ref>https://seaa-web.org/sites/default/files/publications/bseaa-1/BSEAA1-Rew-Styles.pdf</ref>

Wooden {{ill|Column|en|Column|WD=Q4817|lt=pillars}} 20 cm in diameter were erected within its moats.<ref>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/prepareDownload?itemId=info%3Andljp%2Fpid%2F9591536&contentNo=1</ref><ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Wooden {{ill|Chicken|en|Chicken|WD=Q780|lt=roosters}} painted in vermilion lacquer were discovered in the moat surrounding the mound's circular section.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The bird carving is 39 centimeters long.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}} Historians and archaeologists believe these [[:en:roosters|roosters]] might have been part of [[:en:ceremonies|ceremonies]] aimed at praying for the {{ill|Resurrection|en|Resurrection|WD=Q188681|lt=Resurrection}} of the deceased. Similar wooden and clay figures resembling roosters have been found in other early [[Kofun period]] tombs.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Roosters are the {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=animal messengers}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
The birds may symbolize the {{ill|Metempsychosis|en|Metempsychosis|WD=Q1262856|lt=soul's journey}}. These items were found in peat at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-sone site}} in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi City}}, Osaka. The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}
The tomb faces {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}, a sacred mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The [[Kojiki]], says the god [[Ōmononushi]] wanted people to worship him on this mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> There is a complex narrative about {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}} and the establishment of worship in the region.<ref name="Ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}} with some interpreting the god as being {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama}} one of the two gods previously worshipped in the Imperial palace alongside [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="Ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="Hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}} The tomb's direction shows that the Makimuku area's rulers and people respected {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} deeply. Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=religious link}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} * [[Hokenoyama Kofun]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Ancient Kofun in Japan}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}| image=File:Makimuku,ishidukakofun.JPG |coordinates={{Coord|34.54625|N|135.836083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |caption=The three tumuli are surrounded by the Makimuku Katsuyama tumulus (top), the Yatsuka tumulus (bottom left) and the Ishizuka tumulus (bottom right), and the Makimuku Elementary School is located between them.|location={{ill|Sakurai|WD=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11608504|1=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun}}}}
'''Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun''' is a [[Kofun]] found on the premises of [[Makimuku ruins]]. It is a keyhole shaped Kofun.<ref name="mw-2023a">{{Cite web |date=2023-10-16 |title=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun [纏向石塚古墳] Long Barrow : The Megalithic Portal and Megalith Map |url=https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231016234953/https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |archive-date=2023-10-16 }}</ref> It is a Kofun of [[Makimuku ruins]].<ref name="Ki11er-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Ki11er |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.1515/9780824862848 |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>{{rp|249}}
During WWII it was flattened to use as a site for an {{ill|Anti-aircraft warfare|en|Anti-aircraft warfare|WD=Q86739852|lt=Anti-aircraft cannon|1=Anti-aircraft warfare}}.<ref name="mw-2023a" />'
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} and one of the most prominent tombs of its time.<ref name="Ishino-1992a">{{Cite journal |last1=Ishino |first1=Hironobu |last2=石野博信 |date=1992 |title=Rites and Rituals of the Kofun Period |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=19 |issue=2/3 |pages=191–216 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.19.2-3.1992.191-216 |jstor=30234190 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref>
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is 92 meters long. It dates back to the time of [[Himiko]], who led the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamatai kingdom]] in Japan. [[Himiko]] made connections with the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei court|1=Cao Wei}} in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}. She was important in changing {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious|1=Religion in Japan}} and ritual practices.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
It is dated to around 180CE, with the nearby {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|1=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} being dated to around {{ill|200|en|200|WD=Q23813|lt=200AD|1=200}}.<ref>https://seaa-web.org/sites/default/files/publications/bseaa-1/BSEAA1-Rew-Styles.pdf</ref>
It was the first Kofun excavated from [[Makimuku ruins]],<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a">{{Cite web |title=State Formation in Japan: Emergence of a 4th-Century Ruling Elite |url=https://www.routledge.com/State-Formation-in-Japan-Emergence-of-a-4th-Century-Ruling-Elite/Barnes/p/book/9780415596282 |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=115}} and was where some of the earliest {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} was found.<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a" />{{Rp|page=115–116}}
== Religious significance == [[File:Shinshi 001.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Chickens at [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Parishioners believe they are {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=messengers|1=Shinshi}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="D.-2011">{{Cite web |last=D. |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="Ise Shrine-2013">{{Cite web |date=2013-02-15 |title=Ise Shrine (Ise Jingu) - Ise, Japan |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130215040040/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-date=2013-02-15 |access-date=2023-11-11}}</ref><ref name="Olderr-2017">{{Cite book |last=Olderr |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y5gZDgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA171 |title=Symbolism: A Comprehensive Dictionary, 2d ed. |date=2017-02-10 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9067-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Goto-2015">{{Cite book |last1=Goto |first1=Seiko |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VN29CgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA132 |title=Japanese Gardens: Symbolism and Design |last2=Naka |first2=Takahiro |date=2015-10-16 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-41164-2 |language=en}}</ref>]] {{ill|Archaeological record|en|Archaeological record|WD=Q2686349|lt=Archaeological evidence|1=Archaeological record}} indicates rituals were performed in the tomb. Wooden {{ill|Column|en|Column|WD=Q4817|lt=pillars|1=Column}} 20 cm in diameter were erected within its moats.<ref>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/prepareDownload?itemId=info%3Andljp%2Fpid%2F9591536&contentNo=1</ref><ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Wooden {{ill|Chicken|en|Chicken|WD=Q780|lt=roosters|1=Chicken}} painted in vermilion lacquer were discovered in the moat surrounding the mound's circular section.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The bird carving is 39 centimeters long.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}} Historians and archaeologists believe these [[:en:roosters|roosters]] might have been part of [[:en:ceremonies|ceremonies]] aimed at praying for the {{ill|Resurrection|en|Resurrection|WD=Q188681|lt=Resurrection|1=Resurrection}} of the deceased. Similar wooden and clay figures resembling roosters have been found in other early [[Kofun period]] tombs.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Roosters are the {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=animal messengers|1=Shinshi}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
The birds may symbolize the {{ill|Metempsychosis|en|Metempsychosis|WD=Q1262856|lt=soul's journey|1=Metempsychosis}}. These items were found in peat at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-sone site|1=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi City|1=Izumi, Osaka}}, Osaka. The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}
The tomb faces {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, a sacred mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The [[Kojiki]], says the god [[Ōmononushi]] wanted people to worship him on this mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> There is a complex narrative about {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} and the establishment of worship in the region.<ref name="Ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}} with some interpreting the god as being {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} one of the two gods previously worshipped in the Imperial palace alongside [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="Ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="Hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}} The tomb's direction shows that the Makimuku area's rulers and people respected {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} deeply. Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=religious link|1=Mountain worship}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} * [[Hokenoyama Kofun]]

The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}
The tomb faces {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, a sacred mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The [[Kojiki]], says the god [[Ōmononushi]] wanted people to worship him on this mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> There is a complex narrative about {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} and the establishment of worship in the region.<ref name="Ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}} with some interpreting the god as being {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} one of the two gods previously worshipped in the Imperial palace alongside [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="Ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="Hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}} The tomb's direction shows that the Makimuku area's rulers and people respected {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} deeply. Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=religious link|1=Mountain worship}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} * [[Hokenoyama Kofun]]
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Ancient Kofun in Japan}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}| image=File:Makimuku,ishidukakofun.JPG |coordinates={{Coord|34.54625|N|135.836083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |caption=The three tumuli are surrounded by the Makimuku Katsuyama tumulus (top), the Yatsuka tumulus (bottom left) and the Ishizuka tumulus (bottom right), and the Makimuku Elementary School is located between them.|location={{ill|Sakurai|WD=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11608504|1=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun}}}}
'''Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun''' is a [[Kofun]] found on the premises of [[Makimuku ruins]]. It is a keyhole shaped Kofun.<ref name="mw-2023a">{{Cite web |date=2023-10-16 |title=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun [纏向石塚古墳] Long Barrow : The Megalithic Portal and Megalith Map |url=https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231016234953/https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |archive-date=2023-10-16 }}</ref> It is a Kofun of [[Makimuku ruins]].<ref name="Ki11er-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Ki11er |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.1515/9780824862848 |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>{{rp|249}}
During WWII it was flattened to use as a site for an {{ill|Anti-aircraft warfare|en|Anti-aircraft warfare|WD=Q86739852|lt=Anti-aircraft cannon|1=Anti-aircraft warfare}}.<ref name="mw-2023a" />'
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} and one of the most prominent tombs of its time.<ref name="Ishino-1992a">{{Cite journal |last1=Ishino |first1=Hironobu |last2=石野博信 |date=1992 |title=Rites and Rituals of the Kofun Period |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=19 |issue=2/3 |pages=191–216 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.19.2-3.1992.191-216 |jstor=30234190 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref>
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is 92 meters long. It dates back to the time of [[Himiko]], who led the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamatai kingdom]] in Japan. [[Himiko]] made connections with the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|WD=Q320930|lt=Wei court|1=Cao Wei}} in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}. She was important in changing {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|WD=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious|1=Religion in Japan}} and ritual practices.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
It is dated to around 180CE, with the nearby {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|WD=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|1=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} being dated to around {{ill|200|en|200|WD=Q23813|lt=200AD|1=200}}.<ref>https://seaa-web.org/sites/default/files/publications/bseaa-1/BSEAA1-Rew-Styles.pdf</ref>
It was the first Kofun excavated from [[Makimuku ruins]],<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a">{{Cite web |title=State Formation in Japan: Emergence of a 4th-Century Ruling Elite |url=https://www.routledge.com/State-Formation-in-Japan-Emergence-of-a-4th-Century-Ruling-Elite/Barnes/p/book/9780415596282 |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=115}} and was where some of the earliest {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} was found.<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a" />{{Rp|page=115–116}}
== Religious significance == [[File:Shinshi 001.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Chickens at [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Parishioners believe they are {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=messengers|1=Shinshi}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="D.-2011">{{Cite web |last=D. |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="Ise Shrine-2013">{{Cite web |date=2013-02-15 |title=Ise Shrine (Ise Jingu) - Ise, Japan |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130215040040/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-date=2013-02-15 |access-date=2023-11-11}}</ref><ref name="Olderr-2017">{{Cite book |last=Olderr |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y5gZDgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA171 |title=Symbolism: A Comprehensive Dictionary, 2d ed. |date=2017-02-10 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9067-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Goto-2015">{{Cite book |last1=Goto |first1=Seiko |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VN29CgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA132 |title=Japanese Gardens: Symbolism and Design |last2=Naka |first2=Takahiro |date=2015-10-16 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-41164-2 |language=en}}</ref>]] {{ill|Archaeological record|en|Archaeological record|WD=Q2686349|lt=Archaeological evidence|1=Archaeological record}} indicates rituals were performed in the tomb. Wooden {{ill|Column|en|Column|WD=Q4817|lt=pillars|1=Column}} 20 cm in diameter were erected within its moats.<ref>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/prepareDownload?itemId=info%3Andljp%2Fpid%2F9591536&contentNo=1</ref><ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Wooden {{ill|Chicken|en|Chicken|WD=Q780|lt=roosters|1=Chicken}} painted in vermilion lacquer were discovered in the moat surrounding the mound's circular section.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The bird carving is 39 centimeters long.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}} Historians and archaeologists believe these [[:en:roosters|roosters]] might have been part of [[:en:ceremonies|ceremonies]] aimed at praying for the {{ill|Resurrection|en|Resurrection|WD=Q188681|lt=Resurrection|1=Resurrection}} of the deceased. Similar wooden and clay figures resembling roosters have been found in other early [[Kofun period]] tombs.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Roosters are the {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|WD=Q10444973|lt=animal messengers|1=Shinshi}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
The birds may symbolize the {{ill|Metempsychosis|en|Metempsychosis|WD=Q1262856|lt=soul's journey|1=Metempsychosis}}. These items were found in peat at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-sone site|1=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi City|1=Izumi, Osaka}}, Osaka. The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}
The tomb faces {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, a sacred mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The [[Kojiki]], says the god [[Ōmononushi]] wanted people to worship him on this mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> There is a complex narrative about {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} and the establishment of worship in the region.<ref name="Ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}} with some interpreting the god as being {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} one of the two gods previously worshipped in the Imperial palace alongside [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="Ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="Hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}} The tomb's direction shows that the Makimuku area's rulers and people respected {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} deeply. Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|WD=Q93697439|lt=religious link|1=Mountain worship}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} * [[Hokenoyama Kofun]]
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Zenpokoenfun" to "Zenpōkōenfun"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]][[ja:纒向石塚古墳]]
{{Short description|Ancient Kofun in Japan}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}| image=File:Makimuku,ishidukakofun.JPG |coordinates={{Coord|34.54625|N|135.836083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |caption=The three tumuli are surrounded by the Makimuku Katsuyama tumulus (top), the Yatsuka tumulus (bottom left) and the Ishizuka tumulus (bottom right), and the Makimuku Elementary School is located between them.|location={{ill|Sakurai|qid=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11608504|1=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun}}}}
'''Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun''' is a [[Kofun]] found on the premises of [[Makimuku ruins]]. It is a keyhole shaped Kofun.<ref name="mw-2023a">{{Cite web |date=2023-10-16 |title=Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun [纏向石塚古墳] Long Barrow : The Megalithic Portal and Megalith Map |url=https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231016234953/https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53965 |archive-date=2023-10-16 }}</ref> It is a Kofun of [[Makimuku ruins]].<ref name="Ki11er-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Ki11er |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.1515/9780824862848 |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>{{rp|249}}
During WWII it was flattened to use as a site for an {{ill|Anti-aircraft warfare|en|Anti-aircraft warfare|qid=Q86739852|lt=Anti-aircraft cannon|1=Anti-aircraft warfare}}.<ref name="mw-2023a" />'
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is a {{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} and one of the most prominent tombs of its time.<ref name="Ishino-1992a">{{Cite journal |last1=Ishino |first1=Hironobu |last2=石野博信 |date=1992 |title=Rites and Rituals of the Kofun Period |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=19 |issue=2/3 |pages=191–216 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.19.2-3.1992.191-216 |jstor=30234190 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref>
The Makimuku Ishizuka Tomb is 92 meters long. It dates back to the time of [[Himiko]], who led the [[:en:Yamataikoku|Yamatai kingdom]] in Japan. [[Himiko]] made connections with the {{ill|Cao Wei|en|Cao Wei|qid=Q320930|lt=Wei court|1=Cao Wei}} in {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}. She was important in changing {{ill|Religion in Japan|en|Religion in Japan|qid=Q1056672|lt=Japanese religious|1=Religion in Japan}} and ritual practices.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
It is dated to around 180CE, with the nearby {{ill|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|en|Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|qid=Q11608502|lt=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun|1=Makimuku Katsuyama Kofun}} being dated to around {{ill|200|en|200|qid=Q23813|lt=200AD|1=200}}.<ref>https://seaa-web.org/sites/default/files/publications/bseaa-1/BSEAA1-Rew-Styles.pdf</ref>
It was the first Kofun excavated from [[Makimuku ruins]],<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a">{{Cite web |title=State Formation in Japan: Emergence of a 4th-Century Ruling Elite |url=https://www.routledge.com/State-Formation-in-Japan-Emergence-of-a-4th-Century-Ruling-Elite/Barnes/p/book/9780415596282 |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=Routledge & CRC Press |language=en}}</ref>{{Rp|page=115}} and was where some of the earliest {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|qid=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} was found.<ref name="Routledge-CRC-Press-2023a" />{{Rp|page=115–116}}
== Religious significance == [[File:Shinshi 001.jpg|thumb|250x250px|Chickens at [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. Parishioners believe they are {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|qid=Q10444973|lt=messengers|1=Shinshi}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="D.-2011">{{Cite web |last=D. |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="Ise Shrine-2013">{{Cite web |date=2013-02-15 |title=Ise Shrine (Ise Jingu) - Ise, Japan |url=http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130215040040/http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/ise-shrine |archive-date=2013-02-15 |access-date=2023-11-11}}</ref><ref name="Olderr-2017">{{Cite book |last=Olderr |first=Steven |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=y5gZDgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA171 |title=Symbolism: A Comprehensive Dictionary, 2d ed. |date=2017-02-10 |publisher=McFarland |isbn=978-0-7864-9067-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Goto-2015">{{Cite book |last1=Goto |first1=Seiko |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VN29CgAAQBAJ&dq=Amaterasu+rooster&pg=PA132 |title=Japanese Gardens: Symbolism and Design |last2=Naka |first2=Takahiro |date=2015-10-16 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-41164-2 |language=en}}</ref>]] {{ill|Archaeological record|en|Archaeological record|qid=Q2686349|lt=Archaeological evidence|1=Archaeological record}} indicates rituals were performed in the tomb. Wooden {{ill|Column|en|Column|qid=Q4817|lt=pillars|1=Column}} 20 cm in diameter were erected within its moats.<ref>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/view/prepareDownload?itemId=info%3Andljp%2Fpid%2F9591536&contentNo=1</ref><ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Wooden {{ill|Chicken|en|Chicken|qid=Q780|lt=roosters|1=Chicken}} painted in vermilion lacquer were discovered in the moat surrounding the mound's circular section.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The bird carving is 39 centimeters long.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}} Historians and archaeologists believe these [[:en:roosters|roosters]] might have been part of [[:en:ceremonies|ceremonies]] aimed at praying for the {{ill|Resurrection|en|Resurrection|qid=Q188681|lt=Resurrection|1=Resurrection}} of the deceased. Similar wooden and clay figures resembling roosters have been found in other early [[Kofun period]] tombs.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> Roosters are the {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|qid=Q10444973|lt=animal messengers|1=Shinshi}} of [[Amaterasu]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2011-07-28 |title=Power animals |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2011/07/28/power-animals/ |access-date=2023-04-12 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
The birds may symbolize the {{ill|Metempsychosis|en|Metempsychosis|qid=Q1262856|lt=soul's journey|1=Metempsychosis}}. These items were found in peat at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|qid=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-sone site|1=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|qid=Q696412|lt=Izumi City|1=Izumi, Osaka}}, Osaka. The {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato-takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} story mentions a white bird flying between tombs. This story relates to the found items. It shows ancient beliefs about life and the soul.<ref name="Ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250}}
The tomb faces {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, a sacred mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> The [[Kojiki]], says the god [[Ōmononushi]] wanted people to worship him on this mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" /> There is a complex narrative about {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} and the establishment of worship in the region.<ref name="Ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ |title=Shinto: A History |date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|22}} with some interpreting the god as being {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} one of the two gods previously worshipped in the Imperial palace alongside [[Amaterasu]].<ref name="Ellwood-1990a" /><ref name="Hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|22}} The tomb's direction shows that the Makimuku area's rulers and people respected {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} deeply. Buildings at the Makimuku site also face {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} on purpose. This shows their {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=religious link|1=Mountain worship}} to the mountain.<ref name="Ishino-1992a" />
== See also ==
* {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|qid=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} * [[Hokenoyama Kofun]]

revision.comment: Bot: strip HTML comments; consolidate 8 interlanguage link(s) into wikidata link template [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/25113759987|time triggered pipeline]]
